summary: O'Neil critiques 'gotcha' surveys designed to prove public ignorance, using the classic 'does the sun go around the earth' question as her example. She demonstrates that the question is actually frame-of-reference dependent and technically answerable as 'yes' from an Earth-based observational standpoint. The post exposes how these surveys privilege one framing as the only correct answer while ignoring legitimate alternative interpretations. Her imaginary dialogue with a pollster illustrates how forced yes/no responses can misrepresent actual understanding.
key_points: Surveys designed to prove ignorance are often unscientific and obnoxious
The sun-earth question is frame-of-reference dependent, making 'yes' technically defensible
Forced binary answers strip away nuance and can misrepresent genuine understanding
These surveys conflate simplified communication with actual ignorance
money_quotes: Periodically you have people conducting surveys to prove how dumb people are.
I hate these surveys, and I'm usually able to ignore these obnoxious and unscientific nature of them.
It depends on your frame of reference.
If I'm standing on the earth, and I look up in the sky, I will observe the sun going around the earth in a wobbly path.
It would be quite a bit simpler to understand the model of the solar system whereby the earth and other planets revolve around the sun.
key_insight: Questions designed to expose ignorance often reveal more about the survey's oversimplified framing than about respondents' actual understanding.
